costs: Major legislative and curriculum-redesign costs across the entire lower-secondary system
Teacher retraining and redeployment costs amid an already aging, shrinking workforce (F-live-implementation_planning-07)
Multi-year transition management costs to avoid Polish-style double-cohort shortfalls
content.equity_impact: Highest potential equity impact among proposals if fully bundled (curriculum harmonization + anti-sorting choice rules + phased transition), directly targeting the mechanism most strongly associated with Hungary's outlier segregation and SES gap; however, does not by itself resolve Roma segregation, which is documented as a legally distinct issue requiring separate remedies (F-live-polish_reform-08).
expected_benefits: Addresses the structural root of Hungary's outlier between-school segregation and SES achievement gap rather than symptoms
Aligns Hungary with comprehensive-system comparators showing lower between-school variance
Removes multiple early selection gates simultaneously if bundled properly, reducing the four-gate fragmentation problem
content.goal: Raise the statutory age of first academic tracking, converting 6- and 8-year entry points into a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase paired with anti-sorting catchment rules, following the Finnish/Polish structural-delay model.

implementation_steps.action: Amend Act CXC of 2011 to raise statutory first-tracking age and set a multi-year phase-out schedule for 6/8-year entry cohorts (grandfathering existing enrolled students)
Design unified national lower-secondary curriculum spanning ages 10-14 to replace differentiated 4/6/8-year curricula
Draft and enact anti-sorting catchment/choice regulations to prevent informal within-system sorting from replacing formal tracking (addressing Finnish 'emphasized classes' precedent)
Manage phased cohort transition avoiding simultaneous double-cohort competition for places, using Poland's disruption as an explicit negative case
Deploy teacher retraining and redeployment program addressing workforce capacity constraints during transition

mechanisms: Cross-national econometric evidence shows early tracking increases inequality and tends to reduce mean performance, providing the core rationale for delay (F-live-international_comparison-04, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01).

Finland's 1972-77 comprehensive reform delayed tracking from 11 to 16 and was bundled with ability-grouping abolition and curriculum/teacher-training change, showing bundling is necessary for the mechanism to work, not delay alone (F-live-international_comparison-08, F-live-finnish_reform-06, F-live-portuguese_reform-05).

Poland's 2017 reversal (advancing tracking by one year) without phased transition caused acute capacity shortfalls (3,173 unplaced students in Warsaw), demonstrating the transition-design risk that any Hungarian reform must explicitly avoid via phasing (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05, F-live-international_comparison-07).

The 6/8-year tracks are codified in primary legislation, meaning structural delay requires parliamentary amendment, not administrative action, raising the legal bar for enactment (F-live-legal_and_governance-01).

Free school choice is constitutionally protected, creating a legal tension that must be resolved via explicit anti-sorting catchment rules bundled with the tracking-age change, since delay alone without choice regulation may not reduce segregation given documented independent primary-school sorting (F-live-legal_and_governance-02, F-live-portuguese_reform-06, F-live-polish_reform-07).
risks: Unbundled delay (age change without curriculum/choice reform) may fail to reduce inequality, as comparative evidence shows delay works only when bundled (F-live-portuguese_reform-05)
Poorly phased transition risks acute capacity shortfalls analogous to Poland 2017 (F-live-polish_reform-05)
Organized gimnázium-director interest groups with demonstrated political access constitute a concrete veto-player risk to enactment or later reversal (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
Constitutional free-choice guarantee may be invoked to challenge anti-sorting catchment rules, creating legal risk of partial implementation (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

Finland's own recent PISA decline and informal 'emphasized classes' sorting show even a successfully delayed system does not guarantee durable equity or performance gains (F-live-finnish_reform-07, F-live-finnish_reform-08)
Church/private schools outside state maintainer jurisdiction could continue informal early selection, undermining uniform delay (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
content.title: Statutory delay of first academic tracking to age 14+ with unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase
